title: 5 Tahsas — St. Philip & The Assembly
narrative:
narrative": "narrative\": \"The fifth day of Tahsas honors the holy Apostle Philip, whose name in the Greek signifies 'lover of horses,' a name that in the ancient world bespoke nobility and valor; yet the nobility Philip displayed was of a heavenly order, for he brought Nathanael to Christ with the simple invitation 'Come and see,' and it was he who asked the Lord at the Last Supper, 'Show us the Father, and it suffices us,' drawing from Christ the revelation that whoever has seen the Son has seen the Father. Philip labored in the regions of Phrygia and is venerated in the Ethiopian church as one who bore the apostolic proclamation into the uttermost parts of the known world, sealing his testimony at last with the crown of martyrdom.

Saint Saba the Sanctified, whose departure from this life is commemorated on this day, was a great Palestinian monastic father of the fifth and sixth centuries whose name in the Semitic tradition signifies 'old man' or 'elder,' a title of honor in the wisdom-traditions of the ancient East. Saba founded the Great Lavra in the Judean desert that bears his name to this day — Mar Saba — and it became the nursery of theologians and hymnographers who would shape the liturgical tradition of the entire Eastern Church. His life of unbroken prayer in the wilderness between Jerusalem and the Dead Sea made him a living icon of the prophetic tradition: like Elijah before him, he kept watch in the desert for the voice of God.

Clement of Alexandria, the third figure of this day's assembly, was a second-century philosopher-theologian whose Greek name Klemens carries the sense of 'merciful' or 'gentle.' Clement sought to demonstrate to the educated pagan world that the Gospel of Christ was the fulfillment of all true philosophy, that the Logos who became incarnate in Jesus was the same Logos who had scattered seeds of truth throughout the wisdom of the Greeks. His great trilogy — the Protrepticus, the Paedagogus, and the Stromateis — sought to lead the inquiring mind from human wisdom through moral formation to mystical theology, tracing a path that Clement himself saw as a preparation for meeting the incarnate Teacher face to face.

The theological seal of this fifth day of Tahsas is the mystery of invitation and preparation. Philip invites with 'Come and see'; Saba prepares the desert heart to receive the voice of the Beloved; Clement prepares the cultivated mind to recognize the Logos in the manger. All three in their distinct vocations perform the same essential ministry of the pre-Nativity season: they clear the ground of the heart, that the seed of the Word might fall upon prepared soil and bring forth fruit a hundredfold when the great feast of the Incarnation arrives.
